text: In the series of seven concluded tests the start-stop tolerance of journal bearings made of INOR-8 was investigated.
Time of operation ranged from 3 to $379\mathrm{hr}$ .
The largest number of start-stop cycles per test was 260.
No evidence of self-welding was found between INOR-8 surfaces immersed in molten salt at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

Coefficients of dry friction for INOR-8 on INOR-8 and for INOR-8 on stainless steel were found to be fairly constant in the 900 to $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ temperature range, a good average value being 0.25.
A set of four television cameras provides nearly $90\%$ viewing coverage of the cell.
Tests on two INOR-8 loops and one Inconel loop were terminated for metallurgical examination; the results are not yet available.
The second of three sample inserts was removed from loop 9354-4 after 10,000 hr of operation.
Findings on this insert are described in Sec 2.1.
Two new INOR-8 loops were started up with BULT-l4 salt.
The PKP centrifugal pump with Fulton Sylphon bellows-mounted seal has logged 17,878 hr of operation in NaK at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The MF-type centrifugal pump is operating in a region of cavitation in salt 30, and service time of more than 20,000 hr has been recorded to date.
The average upper- and lower-seal leakage rates amounted to 30 and 12 cc/day, respectively.
Total service time is 12,000 hr.
A seal in the annulus between a 5-in.-OD graphite tube and a larger, mating Inconel section will be attempted first.
Bench tests have been completed, and the first experiment is scheduled for insertion in the MTR early in 1960.
The viscosity of the fuel mixture BULT-14 (LiF-BeF $_2$ -UF $_4$ -ThF $_4$ , 67-18.5-0.5-14 mole %) was determined in the temperature range from 550 to $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Comparison with previous results on other salt compositions containing a high percentage of ThF $_4$ shows a maximum deviation of $1.8\%$ at the extremes of the temperature range.

Enthalpy measurements with the BeLT-15 mixture (LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ , 67-18-15 mole %) yielded a heat capacity varying from 0.343 Btu·lb $^{-1}$ . $(^\circ\mathrm{F})^{-1}$ at $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to 0.304 Btu·lb $^{-1}$ . $(^\circ\mathrm{F})^{-1}$ at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

The coefficient $\beta$ was $3.63 \times 10^{-4}/^\circ\mathrm{C}$ between 215 and $315^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; comparison with data obtained from density measurements shows good agreement.
Preliminary results were obtained for heat transfer with BULT-14 flowing in heated Inconel and INOR-8 tubes.

The heat transfer appears to be about half that which would be predicted from earlier studies with molten salts; this discrepancy may result from an uncertainty in the value of the thermal conductivity for BULT-14 used in the analysis of the data.
A $23\%$ difference between the results in the Inconel and INOR-8 tubes cannot be explained, at present.
The second of three INOR-8 corrosion inserts was removed after 10,000 hr from an INOR-8 forced-convection loop.
Weight-loss evaluations indicated the insert to have lost $2.0\mathrm{mg/cm^2}$ , which corresponds to a wall thickness decrease of 0.09 mil, if uniform removal of the wall is assumed.
Two of the loops showed normal attack in the form of intergranular and general voids to a depth ranging from 1 to 14 mils.
The third loop was heavily attacked to depths ranging from 18 to 38 mils.
Fifteen grades of graphite were classified according to their resistance to permeation by molten fluorides at 150 psig at $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in 100-hr tests.

When held in direct contact with graphite for 20 hr at $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , LiF-BeF $_2$ -UF $_4$ (62-37-1 mole %) apparently gettered completely the oxide contaminants in the graphite.

No precipitation was detected radiographically in the LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ -UF $_4$ (67-18.5-14-0.5 mole %) after it had been in contact with graphite for 500 hr at $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in a vacuum.
INOR-8 specimens showed no metallographic indications that carburization had occurred after being in contact with TSF graphite at 1000 psi for 700 hr in salt environment at $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

Four braze materials showed good resistance to fuel 130 (LiF-BeF $_2$ -UF $_4$ , 62-37-1 mole %) during a 5000-hr corrosion test in a thermal-convection loop with a $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ hot-leg temperature.

Corrosion tests of 100-hr duration indicated that the usefulness of titanium-, zirconium-, or beryllium-containing brazing alloys in fuel 130 would be limited in pure nickel or INOR-8 containers at $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Heavy attack due to the transfer of the refractory material to the nickel or INOR-8 container walls was observed.

Under equivalent conditions there is no significant difference between the diffusion coefficients of chromium in INOR-8 and in Inconel; thus the steady-state corrosion rate is predicted to be higher by a factor of $15/7$ in Inconel, since this is the ratio of chromium concentration in the two alloys.
Several grades of extruded graphite, but none of the molded types, show less than 1 vol $\%$ permeation by breeder fuels at 60 to 75 psig and $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
About $740\mathrm{kg}$ of purified fuel mixtures was processed during the quarter.

The modified Fulton Sylphon bellows-mounted seal, $^2$ undergoing test in a PKP type of centrifugal pump, has accumulated an additional 3048 hr of operation since the previous report period, for a total of 17,878 hr.
The pump continues to operate at constant conditions of $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ loop temperature, 2400-rpm shaft speed, and 1200-gpm NaK flow.
An MF type of centrifugal pump has continued in operation² and has logged more than 20,000 hr of continuous operation.

salt 30 at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

Examination of the second insert, although not yet complete, indicates that an average weight loss of $2.0\mathrm{mg/cm}^2$ ( $\pm 3\%$ ) occurred along its 4-in. length.
If uniform removal of surface metal is assumed, this weight loss corresponds to a loss in wall thickness of 0.09 mil.
The first of the inserts, which was removed from the loop after 5000 hr, showed a weight loss of $1.8\mathrm{mg/cm}^2$ ( $\pm 2\%$ ), or a loss in wall thickness of 0.08 mil.
The similarity of these values to the values obtained after 10,000 hr indicates that no significant weight loss occurred after the first 5000 hr of loop operation.

The method used to subject brazing alloys to the corrosive environment in the hot leg of a thermal-convection loop has been described earlier,[6] and corrosion test results on a series of brazing alloys subjected to fuel 130 (LiF-BeF $_2$ -UF $_4$ , 62-37-1 mole %) for 1000 hr at a $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ hot-leg temperature in such a loop have been reported.[7]
The results of a 5000-hr test made under the same conditions are outlined in Table 2.1.6.
A depleted region to a depth of 3 mils was observed along each of the Coast Metals alloy fillets after the test.

Results of earlier corrosion tests on these alloys in $\mathrm{NaF - ZrF_4}$ -base fuels and in liquid metals indicated that this depletion was due to the leaching of the minor constituents boron and silicon from the alloy by the bath, and it appears that boron and silicon are also being leached by fuel 130.
A 10,000-hr thermal-convection-loop test on Inconel and INOR-8 lap joints brazed with the five brazing materials listed in Table 2.1.6 is in progress.

Test results, listed in Table 2.1.7, showed large weight losses for each alloy.

In order to determine the alloying element or elements responsible for the heavy attack, the following static corrosion tests in fuel l30 were conducted at $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ : (1) titanium was tested in containers of titanium, nickel, and INOR-8, (2) zirconium was tested in containers of zirconium, nickel, and INOR-8, and (3) beryllium was tested in a nickel container.

Continuing studies of the phase equilibria in the system NaF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ have established that this system involves 12 invariant equilibrium points.
Of these, ten have been established to within 1 mole % and $5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; these ten are listed in Table 2.2.1.

Three mixtures, NaF-ZrF₄, NaF-LiF, and NaF-LiF-ZrF₄, have been used; of these, NaF-LiF has the largest capacity for ZrF₄, but in this case liquidus temperatures approaching 600°C are encountered at 25 mole % ZrF₄.

Exploratory investigations of the phase equilibria in the system NaF-BeF $_2$ -ZrF $_4$ have shown that NaF-BeF $_2$ (69-31 mole %) affords a solvent for ZrF $_4$ which will accommodate as much as 52 mole % of ZrF $_4$ in solution at temperatures below $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and that no liquidus temperatures higher than $570^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ will be encountered on the composition path between NaF-BeF $_2$ (69-31 mole %) and NaF-BeF $_2$ -ZrF $_4$ (33-15-52 mole %).

The over-all diffusion coefficients obtained for INOR-8 specimens are shown as points on curve 6 of Fig.
At temperatures greater than $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the INOR-8 coefficients are of the same magnitude as the Inconel coefficients.
This means that the rates of chromium diffusion in INOR-8 are approximately 7/15 of the rates in Inconel, at least at higher temperatures.
The ratio 7/15 corresponds to the ratio of the chromium concentrations in the alloys.
At temperatures below $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the INOR-8 coefficients appear to fall considerably below the Inconel coefficients.
This sudden drop might afford a partial explanation of the encouragingly low molten-salt corrosion rates exhibited by INOR-8 within the 500 to $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ temperature range.

Further experiments confirmed the observations reported previously² that $\mathrm{ClF}_3$ in HF causes a drastic reduction in the LiF and $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ solubilities with MSR fuel salt (LiF- $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ -ThF₄-UF₄, 62-36.5-1-0.5 mole %).

The presence of significant amounts of $\mathrm{ClF}_3$ in HF decreased the solubility of LiF from $\sim 100$ to $\sim 20 \, \mathrm{g/kg}$ , and of $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ from 30-40 to $< 5 \, \mathrm{g/kg}$ .
Uranium solubilities were generally about 0.25 g/kg; although significant, they are too low to dissolve all the uranium associated with the dissolved LiF and $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ .
Rare-earth and thorium fluorides are very insoluble in these solutions; rare-earth solubilities were in the range 0.0001 to 0.0005 mole %, based on dissolved salt.

Scouting tests of the solubility of fuel salt (LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ -UF $_4$ , 62-36.5-1-0.5 mole %, plus 0.05 mole % rare-earth fluorides and trace fission products) in NO $_2$ -HF solutions were completed.

Thorium solubilities were generally low, and rare-earth solubilities were mostly about 0.001 to 0.002 mole $\%$ , based on dissolved salt (the most reliable value, from an experiment discussed below, was 50-fold less than this).
With 2.7 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{NO}_2$ (Table 2.3.1) the $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ solubility was unexpectedly high, corresponding almost exactly with the $\mathrm{NO}_2$ content on a mole basis.
The uranium solubilities account for about one-third of the uranium associated with the dissolved salt except with 10 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{NO}_2$ , where it accounts for all of it.
With 5 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{NO}_2$ the total salt solubility is a little over 100 g/liter, which is a reasonable value for processing.
In another experiment with approximately 2 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{NO}_2$ in HF, the solution was decanted from the undissolved excess salt and both were analyzed separately.
The LiF, $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ , uranium, and thorium solubilities were 33, 52, 0.5, and $0.04~\mathrm{g / kg}$ , respectively, in reasonable agreement with the values reported above.

The $\mathrm{NO}_2$ -HF solution dissolved $56\%$ of the LiF, $79\%$ of the $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ , $5\%$ of the uranium, and $62\%$ of the cesium activity, but only $0.086\%$ of the cerium and $0.059\%$ of the promethium.
The much larger samples permitted more accurate determinations of the rare-earth activities than in the experiments reported in Table 2.3.1.

The total rare-earth solubilities were $0.000042$ mole $\%$ calculated from the cerium determination and $0.000029$ mole $\%$ calculated from the promethium determinations, based on dissolved LiF and $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ .

As $\mathrm{NO}_2$ was added to HF, the boiling point rose rapidly to about $56^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ at about 25 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{NO}_2$ and then dropped to the boiling point of $\mathrm{NO}_2$ (21°C) with about 60 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{NO}_2$ , at which point a second phase appeared.
